```
Dick Vitale previously declined a standing invitation from CBS and TNT Sports to call an NCAA Tournament game.

An assist from Charles Barkley is finally making it happen this year.

Vitale and Barkley will call Tuesday night's second First Four matchup in Dayton, Ohio, between Texas and N.C. State on truTV with Brian Anderson and Jenny Dell. It will be the second game this season that Vitale and Barkley have worked together, after the Indiana-Kentucky game in December.

"This is happening because he (Barkley) worked a game on ESPN. Now I'm doing back the favor and giving them a game on their network," Vitale said. "I had been offered chances in the last few years. Sean McManus (the retired chairman of CBS Sports) would call and say, 'You shouldn't end your career without doing an NCAA game, and we'll let you do it on CBS.' I appreciated it so much, but I told him the one thing I wanted on my resume was that I worked my entire career at ESPN. He was surprised but said he respected that."

Vitale served as a studio analyst for ESPN's NCAA Tournament coverage from 1980 through 1990. CBS took over the entire tournament in 1991 and began its partnership with TNT Sports in 2011.

Barkley has been a studio analyst for the NCAA Tournament and Final Four on CBS and TNT Sports since 2011. This is the first year he has appeared on ESPN; "Inside the NBA" airs on the network while being produced by TNT Sports.

"One of my goals for 10, 12 years was to call a game with Dick, and they (their bosses) would never let us do it," Barkley said. "It was awesome to do Indiana and Kentucky, and I think it's going to be even better this time. I've said this before, other than the Olympics, March Madness is the most impressive thing I have been a part of. It's incredible."

The longtime friends jelled quickly during the Dec. 13 game at Rupp Arena, which Kentucky won 72-60. The stakes will be higher for this game, though, with the winner facing sixth-seeded BYU in a West Region first-round matchup in Portland, Oregon.

Vitale said the Kentucky game with Barkley was one of his five greatest moments since becoming a television analyst in 1979. He called ESPN's first college basketball game on Dec. 5, 1979.

"The thing is, it's a lot of quick things that you aren't prepared for," Vitale said. "We just react to the situation. That's what I like: It's not scripted at all."

Barkley - who has contributed to Vitale's Pediatric Cancer Fund - also appreciates that Vitale has come back to the airwaves after four fights with cancer in three years.

"One of the reasons I want to work with him is because we're all so lucky because of basketball, and there's never been a bigger cheerleader of college basketball than him. Basketball has given me everything in my life, so it's pretty special," Barkley said. "Sunday was exciting because the bracket was announced, but Tuesday is the official kickoff when the games begin."

```

```
## Wired headphones were supposed to die with the headphone jack. Instead, they're making a comeback as consumers seek out better sound quality and technology from a simpler time.

When Apple ditched the headphone jacks on iPhones in 2016, I went into exile. I wasn't about to let a giant company dictate my listening habits, so I bought an Android and plugged in for dear life. But eventually, my phone took its last breath in the same exact month that Google - one of the last holdouts - said it was getting rid of headphone jacks on its phones too. It felt like a cosmic sign of defeat. So I went back to iPhone, tossed my wired earbuds in a drawer and joined the Bluetooth hordes.

Maybe I gave up too easily. Recently, a quiet movement has grown in the shadows based on a controversial truth: wired headphones are better than Bluetooth. Sales are through the roof in recent months. You can often get better sound for the money with a wired pair, but it's not just audio snobs either. Wired headphones are a full-blown cultural trend - a resurgence some tie to a broader anti-tech backlash. Whether it's practical, political or aesthetic, one thing is clear. Wired headphones are back.

"I'm converted," says Aryn Grusin, a wired-headphone-loving social worker from Portland, Oregon in the US. A few months ago, she borrowed her fiance's old-fashioned wired earbuds and never looked back. "I just think it feels comforting. I like that it signifies to the world that I'm listening to something."

Grusin isn't alone. After five straight years of declining sales, wired headphone purchases exploded in the second half of 2025, according to the analytics firm Circana, and revenue from wired headphones was up 20% in the first six weeks of 2026. 

"It feels like a lot of people are almost turning on technology because it's becoming so advanced," Grusin says. "I think there's a general group mindset where we're like, 'I don't like how this feels' and we're all kind of returning to the last place we were comfortable."

### 'It's becoming a class thing'

Sound quality can be a big advantage to the wired life, says Chris Thomas, editor at large at the headphone review site SoundGuys. "This is the drum I've been beating for many years now," he says.

Wireless headphones have improved dramatically, according to Thomas, but the best often come from niche brands made for audiophiles. When it comes to mainstream products you'd find in an electronics store, he says you'll still get better sound for the money if you chose the best wired option. Plus, even the finest Bluetooth headphones may not deliver their top audio performance because of bad connections or compatibility issues with your device. "With a wire, you just plug in and it works," he says.

But sound isn't enough to explain the trend. Somehow, it seems Bluetooth has turned deeply unsexy. Don't take my word for it. Ask actor Zoe Kravitz. 

"Bluetooth does not work," Kravitz said in a recent interview, and it's not just headphones, but Bluetooth connections in general. "It's ruining important moments. Imagine the amount of times that you're with someone on a date, you're trying to set a vibe, and then you have to forget the network. On a date!" 

In fact, wired headphones are now a must-have fashion accessory in some circles. There's a popular Instagram account on the subject called Wired It Girls, dedicated to women looking chic and unbothered with cables dangling from their ears, from regular people to celebrities like Ariana Grande and Charli XCX.

Wired headphones have become so ubiquitous among the rich and famous that some see these tangles of plastic and wire as a cultural symbol. One social media user posted a viral tweet with photos of actors Robert Pattinson and Lily-Rose Depp sporting wired earbuds. "It's becoming a class thing," they said. "Wearing wireless 24/7 tells me you don't own any land."

Of course, there's something freeing about untethered, wireless listening. But batteries die at the most inopportune moment. Tiny earbuds get lost. Devices won't pair.

"People say it's easier, but it never feels easier to me," says Ailene Doloboff, a dialogue editor in the film business in Los Angeles. "With Bluetooth it's always one extra step." 

Wired headphones join a list of seemingly obsolete technologies that have roared back in recent years, just as we tumble into the next digital era. People young and old are adopting retro products such as DVDs, cassettes, ancient tube TVs and even typewriters. At a recent concert, I saw a guy in the audience recording the show not with a phone, but a 16mm film camera from the 1970s.

"I don't know why, but we all collectively had this switch. I think the presence of AI is making people more on edge," Grusin says. "Which is ironic, in a way. I'm uncomfortable with technology so I want to use a different piece of technology. But maybe plug-in headphones feel as close to analogue as we can get."

### Dongle trouble

If you go wired, the question is how you'll plug the things in. But you can now buy wired headphones with a built-in USB or Lightning cable connection. Or you can use headphones with the traditional 3.5mm jack via an adaptor for the charging port, often called a "dongle", a word so undignified I spent years refusing to try one.

Apple removed the headphone jack on its phones in 2016 with the launch of the iPhone 7, which many saw as the end of wired listening. But even Apple still hasn't given up on wired headphones. "Oh, we still sell those," the company's chief executive Tim Cook - the man who killed the headphone jack on phones - told my BBC colleague Zoe Kleinman a few years ago. "People still buy them."

I stopped by an Apple store on my way home from the office to grab a cheap pair with a USB connection for myself. An employee told me he's been selling more wired earbuds than ever before. I spent a few days with the wires. I liked the feeling. Being tied to my device made me feel a little more present with my listening, and they also sat more comfortably in my ears than the heavy buds of my Bluetooth set.

But our relationship was short. I've never managed to never lose a pair of Bluetooth earbuds. The case for my AirPods is bulky enough that I can always tell if they're not with me. Not so for the featherlight wired earbuds. They slipped out of my pocket somewhere on the streets of my neighbourhood. I hope they found a more loving home.

Determined, I thought an upgrade might make me more careful. So I visited a specialty headphones store in New York called Audio 46, tucked away in a narrow storefront. Delaney Czernikowski, who reviews headphones for the company's website, greeted me at the counter.

"A lot of people are jumping on the trend. They come in saying 'I think wired is better, I want to try them'," Czernikowski says. "But sometimes they're worried about losing the Bluetooth convenience. I tell them Bluetooth can be very good, you don't have to lose out."

Czernikowski let me try some of the fancier Bluetooth headphones in the store, with incredible sound quality and incredible price tags to match. They were enough to make even the most religious audiophiles drool. "But to be fair, the wired headphones, a lot of them are better and there's a lot more to choose from," she says. "And they do have nicer qualities that aren't weighed down by the need for Bluetooth technology inside."

I planned on grabbing something cheap. With about a minute of convincing, Czernikowski convinced me to try on a pair of $130 (GBP 98) earbuds from a specialty Chinese brand with a thick, gorgeous, braided cable. "Don't compromise," she says. They sound superb for the money, but the BBC doesn't recommend products as part of our commitment to impartiality. So, you'll have to do your own research.

I handed over my credit card, bought a godforsaken USB dongle to go with the headphones and walked outside to plug them in.
```

- Battery independence: No charging required for passive wired headphones. That’s a big plus for travelers, long commutes, and anyone who dislikes carrying yet another cable/charger/power bank.

- Price-to-performance: For a given budget, wired models often deliver better drivers and build quality than similarly priced wireless options. You can get high-end sound without paying for wireless tech.

- Niche trends and influencer effect: Reviews, blogs, and creators highlighting old-school wired models (and affordable high-quality wired options) can spur renewed interest.

Marc-Olivier Frappier ate a lot of roast chicken in restaurants as a kid. He liked the places just fine. Love came later in life.

From his position as chef at Joe Beef and Vin Papillon, restaurants instrumental in elevating Montreal's status as a global food destination in the 2010s, Mr. Frappier, 37, came to admire the humble restaurants that serve simple plates of roast chicken with gravy, French fries and coleslaw all over Quebec.

What locals call rotisseries and barbecues are an unlikely source of inspiration for a chef of Mr. Frappier's renown. Many are part of large chains and designed for takeout. Few are atmospheric.

Yet Mr. Frappier sees rotisseries as treasured heritage.

"They are one of the really true Quebecois restaurants that we have," he said. "I wanted to cook that food."

He does just that at Rotisserie La Lune, the restaurant he and his partners opened in December 2024, months after their other restaurant, Mon Lapin, topped an influential list of Canada's best restaurants for the second year in a row. It is a measure of Canadians' growing respect for rotisseries that one of the country's top chefs would commit to such a homage.

La Lune opened just as it became increasingly common for rotisserie chicken to be mentioned alongside poutine, smoked meat and bagels as a signature dish of Montreal. And chicken-and-gravy joints are only part of the story in Quebec's largest city. Portuguese rotisseries, known for spicy chicken and pastel de nata, are similarly revered.

"It's hard to get sick of chicken here," Mr. Frappier said. "It's part of who we are."

Vanya Filipovic and Marc-Olivier Frappier, the married co-owners of Rotisserie La Lune, which opened in December 2024.

Unlike traditional Quebec rotisseries and barbecues, La Lune roasts meat other than chicken. This is a plate of both chicken and guinea fowl, with a side of fries.

The idea for La Lune took shape on trips Mr. Frappier took to Europe with Vanya Filipovic, his wife and business partner and a celebrated sommelier. Like Mon Lapin, La Lune is a collaboration between the couple and the co-owners Jessica Noel, Alex Landry and Marc-Antoine Gelinas.

Their vision of the rotisserie as Quebec's answer to the brasserie and trattoria is underscored by the kind of soulful, intricate French Canadian cooking Mr. Frappier is known for, even if some of the dishes veer from the standard rotisserie playbook. Standout items from a January meal included guinea fowl terrine, leeks draped with smoked trout fillets and spit-roasted pork glistening under sauce charcutiere studded with sweet pickled ramp bulbs.

Recipe: Coleslaw With Dijon Vinaigrette

Equal care is taken with the typical Quebecois rotisserie items -- roast chicken, fries, coleslaw, chicken soup -- that anchor La Lune's menu. All of it pairs well with wines from the concise, thoughtful list, filled with relatively affordable bottles, primarily from France and Quebec.

"Dining out has become so expensive," Mr. Frappier said. "We think a huge part of the success of La Lune is a half chicken that feeds two people is 37 dollars."

Mr. Frappier was polishing off a 13.75 Canadian dollar (about $10) chicken leg lunch special in one of the snug booths at Chalet Bar-B-Q, a classic Montreal rotisserie and a source of inspiration for La Lune. The small restaurant, wrapped in pine paneling, serves charcoal roasted chicken and doesn't appear to have changed much since 1944, when it opened.

A chicken leg meal at Chalet Bar-B-Q, served with fries, gravy, slaw and a toasted bun, goes for 13.75 Canadian dollars at lunch. The restaurant, which dates to 1944, is a source of inspiration for La Lune.

"They have a very special way of cooking the chicken," he said. "The charcoal ovens here, you can't buy them anymore."

Many customers like the "sauce" -- it's really gravy -- served in small bowls alongside the chicken so much they order two portions, one to drink before the food arrives, according to an exhibit about Montreal restaurants on display at the McCord Stewart Museum.

Chalet is the best preserved and arguably most beloved of the old school Montreal rotisseries. That at least has been the case since 2011, when Laurier BBQ, which first opened in 1936, closed.

Laurier was one of the handful of thriving roast chicken restaurants in Montreal when Helene and Rene Leger opened the first St-Hubert Bar-B-Q in 1951. The business became synonymous with Quebecois chicken in the postwar years, when it was a pioneer with its then-novel television advertisements, promise of consistency and home delivery service that remains particularly attractive during inclement Canadian weather and important hockey games.

Jean-Pierre Leger, Helene and Rene's son, helped expand the chain through franchising after joining the family business in the late 1960s. He was the longtime chief executive when the business was sold for 537 million Canadian dollars in 2016.

Jean-Pierre Leger is a regular at La Lune. He is also the former longtime chief executive of St-Hubert, an iconic chain of Quebecois rotisseries.

Eyebrows were raised in Montreal after the business was sold to an Ontario-based restaurant group that owns Swiss Chalet, a competing -- and in the eyes of many Quebecois, inferior -- rotisserie chain, but Mr. Leger insists the deal was good for Quebec. (One hundred and ten of St-Hubert's 121 locations are in the province, according to the company's website.)

"The head office is still here," Mr. Leger said in an interview over espresso in the Plateau Mont-Royal neighborhood. "Because in Toronto, nobody speaks French."

St-Hubert's influence on Quebec's rotisserie culture is pronounced. One of Mr. Frappier's first restaurant jobs was at a location in Saint-Hyacinthe, his hometown, east of Montreal.

"Whenever I tell people I worked at St-Hubert, there's always someone else in the kitchen who will be like, 'Yeah, me too,'" he said. "It's a common thing."

Francisco Pedro Castanheira tried St-Hubert for the first time when he traveled from Ribatejo, Portugal, to Montreal for Expo 67, a World's Fair held in 1967.

"It was wild to him that you would have to dip chicken into something to give it flavor," said Glenn Castanheira, a grandson of Mr. Castanheira.

Francisco Pedro went on to introduce Portuguese chicken to Montreal. He opened Rotisserie Coco Rico, a takeout restaurant, in 1970, followed by Jano, a sit-down restaurant, in 1974. Both serve chicken with chile-hot piri piri sauce on the same block of Saint-Laurent Boulevard in a once Jewish section of Plateau Mont-Royal now known as Little Portugal.

Coco Rico helped establish Portuguese rotisseries in Montreal.

The restaurants are Montreal institutions and still operated by descendants of Mr. Castanheira, who died in 2003.

"I basically grew up behind the counter," said Glenn Castanheira, who is today the chief of staff to Soraya Martinez Ferrada, Montreal's mayor.

"When people say, 'Hey, you look familiar,' or find out that I grew up with Coco Rico, they always have a story to tell," Mr. Castanheira said.

Glenn Castanheira and Ceu Castanheira, his aunt, at Coco Rico. They are among the descendants of the restaurant's founder, Francisco Pedro Castanheira, who have worked in the restaurant and still operate it. Mr. Castanheira, the restaurant's former general manager, is chief of staff to Montreal's mayor.

The Castanheira family's restaurants helped to establish a style of Portuguese restaurant that is unique to Montreal and expanded the palate of Quebecois rotisseries. Beyond the spicy chicken, Portuguese rotisseries are distinguished by offerings like chorizo sandwiches, roast pork and pastel de nata.

Spatchcocked chickens roast in rotating cages over charcoal fires at Romados and Ma Poule Mouillee, two popular Portuguese rotisseries located a short walk from each other. George Karaglanis, who grew up working in his Greek family's Montreal deli, reopened Romados in 2021 with his wife, Vicki Eliopoulos, eight months after the original owners closed the restaurant.

"Everything is the same," said Mr. Karaglanis, who'd previously served as the restaurant's manager. "We still get fresh chickens in every day. We make our own piri piri."

A half chicken with fries and a salad at Romados, a popular Portuguese rotisserie. Pastel de nata, the Portuguese egg custard tart, is a typical item at Portuguese chicken restaurants in Montreal.

Chickens are cut to order at Romados.

A second Romados is set to open this month in Laval, a Montreal suburb. It will become part of a small but growing number of Portuguese rotisseries outside the Plateau. Campo, a modern Portuguese rotisserie, opened in downtown Montreal in 2016.

"I grew up going to St-Hubert and the rotisseries in the Portuguese neighborhood, but we wanted to bring one for people to enjoy downtown," said Sandra Ferreira, co-owner of Campo, which now has three locations. The restaurant is part of the restaurant company founded by Ms. Ferreira's father, Carlos Ferreira.

Cafe Ferreira, the family's flagship restaurant, is reopening next week after a remodeling, in time to celebrate its 30th anniversary. The upscale Portuguese restaurant was envisioned as a kind of culinary embassy, Ms. Ferreira said. "And if we're going to be ambassadors of Portuguese cuisine, we needed to have a Portuguese chicken restaurant."

But Mr. Frappier's La Lune remains committed to the classic rotisserie model he was first exposed to as a child. Mr. Leger, a La Lune regular, has helped him get the details right.

Mr. Leger offered tasting notes on La Lune's coleslaw, which was inspired by the best features of the two styles -- one creamy, the other vinaigrette -- at St-Hubert.

Mr. Frappier splits his time between his two restaurants, though it can be hard to stay away from La Lune, and the comforting sight of birds browning over flames.

"You know how they have the Netflix fireplace channel?" Mr. Frappier said. "I want a rotisserie chicken channel. I could watch it for hours."

So, I'd like to ask, do you think that La Lune's cultural significance can be seen as a model for other restaurants and cultural institutions? In other words, can the way that La Lune is able to preserve and reinterpret tradition, educate and make heritage cuisine accessible, build community and tell stories, elevate local ingredients and supply chains, influence peers and trends, and shape tourism and reputation be replicated in other contexts?

Yes — La Lune’s approach can absolutely serve as a model for other restaurants and cultural institutions, and many of its core practices are transferable across contexts. Not every place will have the same ingredients, history, or audience, but the underlying principles can be adapted. Key, replicable elements include:

- Rooting innovation in tradition: Honor core techniques and dishes first, then make thoughtful, restrained changes that enhance rather than erase identity. This preserves authenticity while keeping things relevant.

- Clear storytelling: Use menus, service, design, and communications to explain why dishes exist, who the producers are, and what cultural meaning they carry. Storytelling turns meals into cultural experiences.

- Local collaboration and sourcing: Prioritize local producers and ingredients where feasible. This strengthens local economies, highlights terroir, and creates a supply chain story that guests can connect with.

- High technical standards for humble dishes: Treat simple, traditional items with the same rigor as fine-dining plates. Perfect execution signals respect for the cuisine and elevates everyday foods.

- Accessible hospitality and format: Offer shareable plates, approachable descriptions, and service that welcomes novices. Accessibility broadens the audience without diluting authenticity.

- Thoughtful curation (drinks, sides, desserts): Small, well-chosen lists (wines, beers, cocktails, desserts) that complement the menu reinforce coherence and make the experience feel deliberate.

- Community engagement: Host events, collaborate with local artisans and producers, and create programming that brings people together. Make the restaurant a neighborhood hub, not just a destination.

- Measured innovation: Introduce surprises (new techniques, unexpected pairings, seasonal items) that reflect local identity and chef’s voice rather than novelty for novelty’s sake.

- Amplify producers and provenance: Give visibility to farmers, foragers, and makers. That transparency educates diners and strengthens regional food systems.

- Scalability of concept: Adapt the model to different scales — from small neighborhood spots to larger institutions — by keeping the values consistent while changing logistics and scope.

Limitations and considerations:
- Local context matters: What works in Quebec (maple syrup, specific poultry traditions) won’t translate verbatim elsewhere; adaptation is necessary.
- Economic realities: Sourcing local, high-quality ingredients and paying producers fairly can be costly; financial models must be realistic.
- Authenticity vs. appropriation: Institutions should engage respectfully with cultural traditions, especially when those traditions belong to marginalized communities.
- Audience education takes time: Building appreciation and understanding is gradual — patience and consistent practice are required.

In short, La Lune’s model is not a strict blueprint but a set of guiding principles: honor tradition, elevate execution, center local relationships, tell the story clearly, and design an accessible, communal experience. Applied thoughtfully, those principles can help other restaurants and cultural institutions foster cultural preservation, community building, and meaningful culinary innovation.
